item.title: Forget buying a large desktop as this powerful compact mini PC with 32 GB of RAM costs less than $600
item.itunes:title: Forget buying a large desktop as this powerful compact mini PC with 32 GB of RAM costs less than $600
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
GMKtec K11
$600 $800 Save $200

Powered by a capable AMD Ryzen 9 8945HS processor and 32 GB of DDR5 RAM, this mini PC is mighty in performance. There's even an Oculink for connecting a desktop-class GPU!

$600 at Amazon

GMKtec doesn't appear to be shy when discounting its array of mini PCs for promotions such as Prime Day, and this event is no exception. Right now, you can pick up this incredibly powerful GMKtec K11 for just $600. Now, that sounds like a fair chunk of change to part with, but hear me out. I've got a similar model and it's an excellent machine for running Batocera with its AMD Ryzen 9 8945HS processor and 32 GB of DDR5 RAM.

This is a desktop replacement, almost priced as a console.

Why buy this mini PC?

You've probably noticed the GPU in the image above and are wondering why there is a discrete graphics card on the desk alongside the GMKtec K11. It's not for scale, that's for sure! The K11 has an OCuLink port on the front, which can be used to connect a GPU. So, if you're thinking of downsizing from your current desktop system but wish to enjoy similar capabilities thanks to your GPU, the K11 will work well with graphics expansion.

If that wasn't enough already, GMKtec made it so you can upgrade the RAM to 64 GB, should you somehow require that much memory, and this particular SKU has a 1 TB internal SSD, but that can also be expanded too. That's one of the great parts about the K11: you're never really locked into a specific configuration. For connectivity, we're looking at four USB-A, two USB-C, HDMI, DisplayPort, Ethernet, Wi-Fi 6, Bluetooth 5.2, a 3.5mm audio jack, and the aforementioned OCuLink.

I've not had any trouble running a bunch of games with this mobile processor. The Vega graphics are top-notch notch and while you won't be pushing QHD or UHD resolutions with the latest Steam games, the OCuLink port opens up a world of possibilities with the right expansion card. Depending on what you plan to use the K11 for, it would make for a great desktop PC replacement, retro gaming box, or even the start of your very own self-hosting home lab.

item.title: This cloud-based writing app is the ultimate world-building tool
item.itunes:title: This cloud-based writing app is the ultimate world-building tool
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Whether you’re writing a book or running a tabletop role-playing game, world-building can be a huge challenge. If you’re like me, it’s also one of the most fun and creative parts of the storytelling process, but there’s still a lot to keep track of.

I’ve tried a variety of tools over the years for managing my world-building, including plain old paper binders and cluttered Google Drive folders, but my search finally came to an end when I discovered Campfire.

Campfire: The writing app designed for world-building

It has a ton of features that are great for running TTRPG campaigns

Campfire is a cloud-based writing app for web browsers, Windows, and macOS. There’s also a free mobile app, but it’s much more limited than the regular desktop version. While Campfire isn’t available natively on Linux, I’ve had a great experience with the web version, which functions just like the desktop app.

There’s a lot to love about Campfire — it works across all of my devices, offers a ton of customization, includes more features than I know what to do with, and comes at a reasonable price. Unlike most other paid writing apps, Campfire is broken down into “modules” you can purchase (or subscribe to) individually, meaning you only pay for what you need.

Every module has a limited free trial version, allowing you to determine which ones work best for you before making a purchase. Some, like the Manuscript module, are designed for writing books, but most of the modules are functionally world-building tools. There are a handful I’ve found especially helpful for designing, organizing, and tracking my fictional worlds. Campfire may be intended for authors, but these tools are also great for Dungeons & Dragons or any kind of TTRPG campaign.

Character profiles, relationships, and arcs

Campfire makes it easier to keep track of all those NPCs

If you’ve ever run a TTRPG campaign, you’re probably familiar with one of my biggest D&D struggles: keeping track of everyone’s characters and NPCs. Things get especially tricky when you’re running a long campaign where characters’ stats, inventories, relationships, and goals change over time.

Campfire has a few tools that have really helped me out on that front: the Characters, Relationships, and Arcs modules. These three tools allow you to easily keep track of just about everything you need to know about your characters.

The Characters module is pretty straightforward. Here, you can create customized character profiles with the information you need, including images, stats, backstory, skills, and more. I suggest creating a blank profile that’s customized to your liking, then duplicating it to use as a template for all your characters.

The Relationships and Arcs modules go hand-in-hand with the Characters module, allowing you to map out connections between characters and plot events. Both modules automatically connect to the Characters module, so you can add cards for your existing characters to a relationships web, family tree, or character arc. Conveniently, the Arcs module also connects directly to the Timeline module, where you can keep track of plot events.

Timelines and Calendars in Campfire

Map out your world's history and yearly calendar with these tools

Outlining is a vital part of my storytelling process, whether it’s for creative writing or a TTRPG. So, the Timelines module was one of the first I tried out in Campfire. It’s exactly what you’d expect: a customizable timeline with event cards you can move around to lay out your plot, outline major historical events, or track campaign events.

Of course, you could always do that on paper, but it’s really convenient to have it right there in my writing app and easily accessible from all my devices. The Timeline module connects to the Arcs module, as well, so you can sync up important plot points to character arcs.

The Calendars module is similar, but functions more as a mechanical tool than a literary one. You can use a standard calendar or create your own, with customized months, weeks, days, seasons, lunar patterns, holidays, and more. This is helpful as a background reference in longer fiction or TTRPGs where something like a major holiday could be crucial for the timing of a key plot event (although fair warning, it’s really hard to name your own days and months).

Built-in map management

While one feature is missing, Campfire has basic map tools

What TTRPG or fantasy world is complete without a good map? Campfire has a Maps module that allows you to conveniently access your world maps in the same place as your writing or other world-building information. Admittedly, I still prefer sketching out maps on my iPad, but that’s not an option for everyone, and I don’t often change my maps beyond my basic reference sketch.

With that said, if you are the type of world-builder who likes to have detailed maps you can easily update with important locations and details, Campfire’s Maps module will be a welcome feature. You can’t create maps in it, though. You have to upload an existing map or import one from Inkarnate, one of many websites where you can design fantasy maps from scratch.

To be honest, I wish you could create maps directly in Campfire, so this feature is a bit lackluster for me, but it’s nice to have some basic map-related tools at least. If you have a map ready to go, you can upload it to Campfire and add pins, regions, labels, measurements, and various icons and decorations.

Magic, Species, and Languages

Dive deeper into your world's society and culture in Campfire

Anyone who has tried to create a homebrewed TTRPG world knows that creating a magic system can be seriously challenging. If your world also has fictional creatures, non-human characters, and constructed languages, that adds a whole new layer of complexity.

Campfire’s Magic, Species, and Languages modules can help you organize all of your ideas on these core cultural and social aspects of world-building. They also offer a bit of a framework to help you get started. For instance, the default template for a new magic system includes traits like magic source, cost, limitations, and history. You might want to just jump right into creating spells or cursed objects, but these elements are good to consider if you want to really weave your magic system into your larger world.

Speaking of weaving things into your world, the Languages module comes in handy for world-builders who enjoy the creative challenge of creating a constructed language. It functions as a dictionary, allowing you to create individual entries for each word in your fictional language. I would like to see additional tools added for things like grammar, but you can always store those notes in the Research or Encyclopedia modules.

Lastly, the Species module functions similarly to the Characters module, but is geared more toward natural history. The default template is designed for creating fictional plants and animals, but I’ve also tweaked it for creating non-human character species (i.e., elves or goblins). If you want something a little more freeform for tracking similar info, I also suggest checking out the Encyclopedia module, which is like a wiki for your world.

Getting started with world-building in Campfire

Campfire certainly isn’t for everyone, but it has been perfect for my needs. It's designed for creative writing and world-building, which is great for fantasy and sci-fi. However, if you want a more streamlined writing tool for a manuscript or short stories, you might want to check out Scrivener or Ellipsus instead (just two of many great options available for writers). You can also try out Campfire for free (with no time limits) before buying or subscribing to any modules. It’s worth noting that even if you change your mind and unsubscribe from a module, you’ll still be able to view whatever you’ve already created.

I suggest starting with the Characters, Timelines, and Encyclopedia modules for a well-rounded introduction to the basics. The Encyclopedia module can serve as an all-purpose hub for notes if you don’t need the more granular organization that the Relationships, Arcs, Maps, Species, and Magic modules offer.

Overall, Campfire has become my preferred alternative to paper binders of world-building notes or less organized apps like Google Drive. If you’ve also been searching for a hub for all your world-building needs, you can’t go wrong trying out Campfire.

item.title: Not even Black Friday can match this Prime Day discount, as LG's 27-inch UltraGear monitor drops to its lowest price
item.itunes:title: Not even Black Friday can match this Prime Day discount, as LG's 27-inch UltraGear monitor drops to its lowest price
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
LG UltraGear 27GS75Q-B
$190 $280 Save $90
Resolution
1440p
Refresh Rate
180Hz
Screen Size
27 inches
Display Technology
IPS
Response Time
1ms

A 27-inch monitor that's built for entertainment, delivering impressive visuals at a fantastic price. Prime Day knocks it down to its lowest price ever at just $190. Act quickly, because this deal won't be around for long.

$190 at Amazon

Prime Day is finally here, and while we'd usually caution you to wait it out for Black Friday, these are some of the best discounts we've seen in a while. And it's not just the pricier stuff either, because this LG Ultragear 27-inch gaming monitor is down to its lowest price, making it perfect for someone who wants a new 1440p monitor.

It has a 180Hz refresh rate that can overclock to 200Hz, along with a 1ms response time, great colors, and a price that comes in at just $190. If this sounds like what you're looking for, now's the time. Remember, this Prime Day event is only for two days, so you'll want to try and act quickly because you won't have long to decide.

Don't miss out

Credit: LG

In addition to the above, you get deep colors with a 99% sRGB color gamut and support for HDR10. Furthermore, LG also throws in support for Nvidia G-Sync and AMD FreeSync technologies, which can enhance the gaming experience when connected to supported products like PCs, game consoles, and PC gaming handhelds.

There are even modes unique to the display to accommodate and enhance different games you might play. The connectivity is also quite good with two HDMI and one DisplayPort — so you can connect multiple devices to one display to make this your main entertainment hub. The monitor even offers adjustments for height, tilt, and pivot.

The LG Ultragear delivers on many levels and does it for a price that can't be ignored. So if you've been thinking about a new 1440p monitor, grab this one before it's gone.

item.title: Arduino has just been acquired by Qualcomm, and they're already launching a new product that runs Linux
item.itunes:title: Arduino has just been acquired by Qualcomm, and they're already launching a new product that runs Linux
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
In a shock move, Qualcomm has acquired Arduino, the open-source hardware and software company. The move marks one of Qualcomm's most significant steps yet toward expanding its influence beyond smartphones and computing, and into the rapidly growing markets of Internet of Things (IoT), edge computing, and STEM education. What's more, the two companies are already launching their first product: the Arduino UNO Q.

The Arduino UNO Q packs Qualcomm's Dragonwing QRB2210 (matching the part number of the company's RB1 platform) alongside a low-power STM32U5 MCU. It starts at $44 for 2 GB RAM and 16 GB of eMMC, and goes to $59 for 4 GB RAM and 32 GB eMMC.

Qualcomm and Arduino have both stated that they are committed to openness, and schematics and design files for the UNO Q will be released under a CC BY-SA 4.0 license. As well, the new Arduino App Lab, App CLI, Bricks and example software will be released under GPL3 and MPL licenses. These licenses are consistent with Arduino's past releases.

Arduino is an industry leader in open-source hardware

And it seems like it'll stay that way

Arduino, founded in 2005 and headquartered in Monza, Italy, has become synonymous with accessible electronics education and rapid prototyping. Its boards and software are at the heart of millions of DIY projects, classrooms, and research labs worldwide. With an estimated 33 million active users, Arduino has built one of the largest open-source hardware ecosystems on the planet, and one that Qualcomm now seeks to bring under its technological umbrella.

For Qualcomm, best known for its Snapdragon system-on-chip (SoC) platforms, this acquisition represents a strategic expansion beyond mobile and automotive into a much broader developer and education market. According to Qualcomm, "Arduino will retain its independent brand, tools, and mission," though users will be provided with a "a clear path to commercialization" thanks to Qualcomm.

While the acquisition is certainly unexpected, it makes a lot of sense. Arduino has long been a gateway for engineers and developers entering the embedded systems world, and Arduino boards are found literally everywhere: from classrooms to the homes of DIY enthusiasts, most people in the tech sphere have at least heard of Arduino. With this acquisition, Qualcomm gains a direct line to education and maker markets, provides a platform for rapid IoT and edge AI prototyping, and can enhance its own open-source developer outreach.

In a sense, this acquisition allows Qualcomm to better compete with the likes of Nvidia's Jetson boards, Raspberry Pi, and even Espressif's ESP32. After all, Qualcomm is no stranger to developer boards like these, and the company's "DragonBoard" series with the 410c and the 820c (which was a prototype and never widely released) didn't really take off in the same way that those competitors did.

Arduino's success has always stemmed from its transparency, vendor-neutrality, and collaborative development model, and it appears that Qualcomm, at least for now, doesn't want that to change. Massimo Banzi, a co-founder of Arduino, said that "By joining Qualcomm Technologies we'll bring cutting-edge AI tools to our community while staying true to what has always mattered most to us."

The Arduino UNO Q is the most interesting Arduino board yet

The most affordable Arduino board to run Linux

Credit: Source: Qualcomm

Arduino's UNO Q is particularly interesting as it runs Debian, and can be used as its own device without a computer through the USB-C port. While Qualcomm did not share whether it used DisplayPort over USB-C, the illustration the company shared of this particular use-case shows the UNO Q plugged into a USB-C hub. The Dragonwing QRB2210 has some pretty beefy specs for a board like this:

  • Quad Cortex-A53 @ 2.0 GHz
  • Dedicated DSP
  • Adreno 702 @ 845 MHz
  • 720 × 1680 at 60 Hz
  • 2x ISP
  • 1080p 30fps 8-bit HEVC (H.265)/H.264 encode and decode
    • Concurrency: 1080p30 decode + 720p30 encode

  • Qualcomm FastConnect 7800 (2.4 GHz only)
  • Qualcomm Sensing Hub

The only other powerful Arduino board capable of running Linux is the Arduino Portenta X8, which cost almost $250 at launch in 2023. It packed the NXP i.MX 8M Mini (a quad-core Cortex-A53), up to 4 GB of LPDDR4 RAM, and 16 GB eMMC storage. This is the first low-cost board from Arduino that can run Linux in a decade, as the only other low-cost options from the company were the Arduino Yun and Arduino Tian, launched in 2013 and 2015 respectively. The Arduino Tian was powered by a Qualcomm Atheros AR9342 MIPS processor, so a Qualcomm-powered Arduino board isn't a totally new thing.

Hardware wise, the UNO Q has practically everything you could ever want from a development board for prototyping and building new hardware. It has a serial programmer, Wi-Fi and Bluetooth support, an LED matrix built-in to the board, Arduino UNO-specific headers, SPI 3v3 connectivity, a Qwiic 4-pin JST SH port, and more headers for further connectivity.

Credit: Source: Qualcomm

The Arduino App Lab is an all-new IDE for developing on Arduino, which you can run on the UNO Q itself or on a computer with the UNO Q connected. You can combine your usual Arduino sketches, Python scripts, and AI models, and you can run programs on the STM32U585 running Zephyr OS as a part of your development workflow.

Qualcomm's acquisition of Arduino has come as a surprise, but the hardware that the two have built together looks to be incredibly powerful. There's a lot that the UNO Q is capable of, and you can use it as a programmable SBC dedicated to building new projects, or simply as a computer, all while deploying your own local AI models and building your own devices with it.

If you're lookinmg to buy one of the new Arduino UNO Q boards, the 2 GB RAM and 16 GB storage option is available for purchase today, and will ship by October 25th. The 4 GB RAM and 32 GB storage option will be available for purchase in November, and will ship before the end of the year.

item.title: I repurposed my old router into a Wi-Fi extender
item.itunes:title: I repurposed my old router into a Wi-Fi extender
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Wireless connectivity is so ubiquitous today that I sometimes forget it wasn't always like that. Then I remember that I've lived in houses where I needed a Wi-Fi extender to get a consistent signal in the room above the one with the router in, and that many people are likely in similar situations. If that sounds familiar, and you've got an old router lying around, you can turn it into a Wi-Fi repeater as a temporary fix if your router is compatible.

That said, theory doesn't matter if it doesn't work in practice, and I thought I'd set up a Wi-Fi repeater for a few days and see how much has changed in the decade since living in that building. And you know what, it's a lot better than it used to be, and having better wireless coverage is never a bad thing.

Some routers make this easier than others

And if your router is old enough you might need custom firmware or not be able to do this at all

When I last used Wi-Fi extenders, you couldn't enable this functionality in consumer routers and Wi-Fi speeds were slow. Wireless connectivity has improved substantially since then, as have the features bundled in by manufacturers, and it's often trivial to set up an old router in Wi-Fi extender or repeater mode. Sometimes it's bridge mode, and you'll have to check on your specific router to find out which it is.

I couldn't find my old dual-band routers; likely, they're in a box in the garage that was never unpacked after we moved. But I did have a couple of GL.iNet travel routers handy, designed to be used as Wi-Fi repeaters to give you secure wireless connectivity even if you're using a public wireless AP, like in a café or hotel. I set one up upstairs, about half the distance between my main router and the furthest point of the house, and got to testing.

Credit: Source: GL.iNet
GL.iNet GL-BE3600
$170 at Amazon $150 at Gl.iNet

Performance won't be amazing

But it's better than having no wireless coverage at all

I've been testing this configuration for a few days now, and I don't hate it as much as I thought I would. Part of that is the modern Gl.iNet router that I've been using in Wi-Fi repeater mode is, well, modern. The last time I used a Wi-Fi repeater before this was over a decade ago, when mesh networking was still firmly in the enterprise realm and the only way to get it working on home networks was custom router firmware or Asus' AiMesh.

Before you set up Wi-Fi repeating mode, I suggest you do a few speed tests while connected to that router's Wi-Fi in access point mode. That's to get a baseline of what to expect, or more precisely, a baseline of twice what you can expect, because once it's in Wi-Fi repeater mode, your speeds will be halved (at best). If your router gives 400 Mbps when in AP mode, expect 180 to 200 Mbps when in repeater mode, or potentially less depending on the amount of interference in your home.

That's the problem I see with trying to offer piecemeal solutions for Wi-Fi coverage like this. The theory is sound, but everyone's home is different, making setting realistic expectations tricky. But also, those speeds are more than enough for basic browsing or working online, making it less of an issue than a decade ago when Wi-Fi speeds were lower.

And there are other issues

The thing about Wi-Fi repeaters that annoys me isn't the slowdown, because I can handle that if I know what's happening. It's the other issues, like the ones that come from trying to run two access points with different SSIDs. When the Wi-Fi repeater is between your device and the main router, you know what to expect. But when you're between the two Wi-Fi access points, your mobile device doesn't know which one to pick, and that could mean you're losing out on potential performance.

A mesh network with proper handoff built into the firmware would solve this, but if you keep using a Wi-Fi extender, it's better if the devices connected to it are stationary. your phone is fine if it gets too far from the router, as it'll start using mobile data to provide consistent coverage. With laptops, you'll manually switch between the two SSIDs, but there will be parts of your home where one Wi-Fi signal is low enough to auto-switch to the other SSID.

It's annoying more than anything else. I still don't suggest setting the same SSID settings on the Wi-Fi repeater and Wi-Fi router, because without proper hand-off systems in the firmware, it'll blip when switching between the two, and that gets annoying if you're sitting between the two signals.

Using a router as a Wi-Fi repeater is a temporary fix

I wouldn't want to run this networking setup long term, because it has plenty of drawbacks, but it's fine for a quick fix while you develop a more permanent solution. Modern Wi-Fi is often fast enough that halving the speed with the Wi-Fi repeater doesn't meaningfully affect basic browsing tasks, but heavier use will bog down the airwaves.

Modern Wi-Fi is often fast enough that halving the speed with the Wi-Fi repeater doesn't meaningfully affect basic browsing tasks, but heavier use will bog down the airwaves.
item.content:encoded:
Wireless connectivity is so ubiquitous today that I sometimes forget it wasn't always like that. Then I remember that I've lived in houses where I needed a Wi-Fi extender to get a consistent signal in the room above the one with the router in, and that many people are likely in similar situations. If that sounds familiar, and you've got an old router lying around, you can turn it into a Wi-Fi repeater as a temporary fix if your router is compatible.

That said, theory doesn't matter if it doesn't work in practice, and I thought I'd set up a Wi-Fi repeater for a few days and see how much has changed in the decade since living in that building. And you know what, it's a lot better than it used to be, and having better wireless coverage is never a bad thing.

Some routers make this easier than others

And if your router is old enough you might need custom firmware or not be able to do this at all

When I last used Wi-Fi extenders, you couldn't enable this functionality in consumer routers and Wi-Fi speeds were slow. Wireless connectivity has improved substantially since then, as have the features bundled in by manufacturers, and it's often trivial to set up an old router in Wi-Fi extender or repeater mode. Sometimes it's bridge mode, and you'll have to check on your specific router to find out which it is.

I couldn't find my old dual-band routers; likely, they're in a box in the garage that was never unpacked after we moved. But I did have a couple of GL.iNet travel routers handy, designed to be used as Wi-Fi repeaters to give you secure wireless connectivity even if you're using a public wireless AP, like in a café or hotel. I set one up upstairs, about half the distance between my main router and the furthest point of the house, and got to testing.

Credit: Source: GL.iNet
GL.iNet GL-BE3600
$170 at Amazon $150 at Gl.iNet

Performance won't be amazing

But it's better than having no wireless coverage at all

I've been testing this configuration for a few days now, and I don't hate it as much as I thought I would. Part of that is the modern Gl.iNet router that I've been using in Wi-Fi repeater mode is, well, modern. The last time I used a Wi-Fi repeater before this was over a decade ago, when mesh networking was still firmly in the enterprise realm and the only way to get it working on home networks was custom router firmware or Asus' AiMesh.

Before you set up Wi-Fi repeating mode, I suggest you do a few speed tests while connected to that router's Wi-Fi in access point mode. That's to get a baseline of what to expect, or more precisely, a baseline of twice what you can expect, because once it's in Wi-Fi repeater mode, your speeds will be halved (at best). If your router gives 400 Mbps when in AP mode, expect 180 to 200 Mbps when in repeater mode, or potentially less depending on the amount of interference in your home.

That's the problem I see with trying to offer piecemeal solutions for Wi-Fi coverage like this. The theory is sound, but everyone's home is different, making setting realistic expectations tricky. But also, those speeds are more than enough for basic browsing or working online, making it less of an issue than a decade ago when Wi-Fi speeds were lower.

And there are other issues

The thing about Wi-Fi repeaters that annoys me isn't the slowdown, because I can handle that if I know what's happening. It's the other issues, like the ones that come from trying to run two access points with different SSIDs. When the Wi-Fi repeater is between your device and the main router, you know what to expect. But when you're between the two Wi-Fi access points, your mobile device doesn't know which one to pick, and that could mean you're losing out on potential performance.

A mesh network with proper handoff built into the firmware would solve this, but if you keep using a Wi-Fi extender, it's better if the devices connected to it are stationary. your phone is fine if it gets too far from the router, as it'll start using mobile data to provide consistent coverage. With laptops, you'll manually switch between the two SSIDs, but there will be parts of your home where one Wi-Fi signal is low enough to auto-switch to the other SSID.

It's annoying more than anything else. I still don't suggest setting the same SSID settings on the Wi-Fi repeater and Wi-Fi router, because without proper hand-off systems in the firmware, it'll blip when switching between the two, and that gets annoying if you're sitting between the two signals.

Using a router as a Wi-Fi repeater is a temporary fix

I wouldn't want to run this networking setup long term, because it has plenty of drawbacks, but it's fine for a quick fix while you develop a more permanent solution. Modern Wi-Fi is often fast enough that halving the speed with the Wi-Fi repeater doesn't meaningfully affect basic browsing tasks, but heavier use will bog down the airwaves.

item.title: You won't believe the new all-time low Prime Day price for this 6-core AMD CPU
item.itunes:title: You won't believe the new all-time low Prime Day price for this 6-core AMD CPU
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
AMD Ryzen 5 5500
$61 $75 Save $14
Cores
6
Threads
12
Architecture
Zen 3
Process
7nm
Socket
AM4

The Ryzen 5 5500 is a low-end AMD CPU that has six cores, 12 threads, and 16MB of L3 cache, half that of its higher-end but more expensive counterpart, the Ryzen 5 5600.

$61 at Amazon

AMD's Ryzen 5 5500 launched in 2022 for $159 with six cores and 12 threads, offering plenty of performance for a gaming PC or one for a mixture of work and play. Although an AM4 processor that supports DDR4 RAM, this chip shouldn't be overlooked if you're looking for an affordable way to build a new system or upgrade from an older AMD CPU. For Prime Day, it's discounted to just $61. I recall a time when anything above four cores was viewed as a luxury, and here we are with a 6-core CPU for almost $60.

What makes this an epic deal

The processor and graphics card are usually the most expensive parts of any system. Depending on what you need the PC for, it's possible to use an AMD Ryzen 5 or Intel Core Ultra 5 and enjoy a pleasant experience without any sluggishness. You could pair an AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT GPU with this processor and still be able to enjoy butter-smooth gaming at higher resolutions, though I would suggest maybe looking at the also discounted AMD Ryzen 7 7800X3D if building a 4K gaming PC from scratch.

This $61 Ryzen 5 5500 is based on AMD's Zen 3 architecture, and although it doesn't have an "X" suffix, the CPU is fully unlocked and can be boosted through activating AMD's Precision Boost Overdrive (PBO) feature and ensuring XMP or EXPO is enabled for your system memory. And because this CPU has a relatively low TDP of just W, you won't need range-topping liquid cooling to keep temperatures in check. Simply install the chip on an AM4 motherboard, enable EXPO and PBO, and enjoy your favorite games.

We'd typically recommend a mini PC or some other form factor instead of building a PC if on a tight budget, but when you come across discounts such as this, it's difficult to recommend against buying the 5500 and putting together an impressive PC that can be upgraded further down the line should you eventually require something with slightly more power. And that's what's so great about the AM4 platform. AMD is still releasing new processors so there's plenty of SKUs to choose from without requiring a new motherboard.

Need an AMD AM5 processor?

There's a great Prime Day deal for you too!

If you're already rocking an AM5 motherboard or wish to build a slightly more modern PC, Amazon has you covered with these two great Amazon Prime Day deals. First, we've got the AMD Ryzen 9 7900X with its 12 cores and 24 threads. It's an absolute beast, unlocking DDR5 RAM and PCIe 5.0 support with scope to upgrade to the latest and greatest from AMD. This is an excellent choice for those requiring impressive performance at a reasonable price.

But if you are into gaming and wish to build a 4K-capable system, the aforementioned Ryzen 7 7800X3D is where you should land. $338 is a fair price to pay for a modern CPU with AMD's 3D V-Cache technology, which significantly improves in-game performance and pairs well with more powerful graphics cards.

AMD Ryzen 9 7900X
$285 $549 Save $264
Brand
AMD
Cores
12
Threads
24
Architecture
Zen 4
Process
TSMC 5nm

With 12 cores, the Ryzen 9 7900X is the lighter weight flagship CPU of the Ryzen 7000 series. It's great for both multicore workloads and gaming, and while it's not as fast as the 7950X, it's quite a bit cheaper.

$285 at Amazon
AMD Ryzen 7 7800X3D
$338 $449 Save $111
Brand
AMD
CPU Model
Ryzen 7 7800X3D
Cores
8
Threads
16
Architecture
Zen 4

AMD's Ryzen 7 7800X3D CPU is the company's cheapest model with 3D V-Cache and can deliver gaming performance on par or even better than that of the Ryzen 9 7950X.

$338 at Amazon

item.title: Prime Big Deal Days exclusive: Unbeatable water filter savings for the whole family
item.itunes:title: Prime Big Deal Days exclusive: Unbeatable water filter savings for the whole family
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Fall 2025’s Prime Big Deal Days is the perfect time to jump on some amazing sales and invest in home essentials that will support your long-term health. What better way to commit to your well-being than with everyday, healthy hydration? From October 7-8 Waterdrop Filter is offering up to $700 off its premium water purification systems, ranging from its high-end, flagship under-sink systems that can support large families to its ultra-convenient countertop models designed for small spaces.

With cozy season just around the corner, Waterdrop Filter can help you elevate your seasonal beverages: instant, steaming-hot tea; hot cocoa for the kids made with guaranteed pure water; fresh, immunity-boosting citrus water to help ward off circulating flu bugs; ice-cold filtered water to keep you going through your fall workout challenge.

Waterdrop Filter is a long-trusted brand that has dedicated itself to water safety and developing innovative water purification systems to enhance your everyday life. Pure, great-tasting water shouldn’t be a luxury — it’s a necessity that everyone should have access to. Not only is Waterdrop Filter focused on providing healthier water, but it’s also on a mission to decrease plastic waste, and to date has replaced a staggering 1 billion+ single-use plastic water bottles.

Why pure drinking water matters this season

We all know what’s lurking around the corner in the fall and winter: the dreaded cold and flu bugs that circulate when we all move inside for the season. If you want to support a healthy immune system, start with the basics: pure filtered water.

Waterdrop Filter products use an advanced, multi-step Reverse Osmosis (RO) filtration system to reduce large particles, reduce dissolved solids and contaminants, and improve your water’s taste with a post-carbon filter. You end up with safe, better-tasting water with fewer contaminants. Whether you’re preparing your baby’s bottle with formula, cooking a seasonal soup, or indulging in a rainy afternoon Americano, Waterdrop Filter provides premium filtration for the highest-quality, guaranteed pure water every time.

Waterdrop Filter’s premium under-sink RO systems

Waterdrop Filter's X12 Pro RO System

Credit: Waterdrop Filter

If your family refuses to compromise on convenience and the quality of its drinking water, Waterdrop Filter’s flagship X12 Pro RO under-sink system is the perfect fit. The tankless RO system easily slides under your sink, with dual flow that connects to your main faucet as well as the countertop-integrated RO faucet. From your existing faucet, you can now enjoy five-stage filtered water for everyday activities such as washing dishes and rinsing fruits and vegetables. From your new X12 Pro faucet you have the purest, mineral-fortified drinking water that has gone through Waterdrop Filter’s superior 11-stage under-sink RO system.

Busy families will appreciate the speedy, 1200GPD rapid flow that can fill a cup in a mere three seconds. Whether you’re filling a large pot of water for pasta or prepping the kids’ water bottles before school, using the RO faucet won’t slow you down. Unlike traditional RO systems, the X12 Pro was built with both performance and sustainability in mind, providing a 3:1 water ratio that reduces waste water while maintaining a quick flow.

Waterdrop Filter X12 Pro
$999 $1399 Save $400
Filtration stages
11
Flow speed
1200GPD
Instant hot water
No

The Waterdrop X12 Pro RO under-sink system delivers convenience and quality with dual-flow filtration, providing five-stage filtered water from your main faucet and 11-stage mineral-fortified drinking water from its dedicated RO faucet. With rapid 1200GPD flow and a sustainable 3:1 water ratio, it’s designed to keep up with busy families while minimizing waste.

See at Amazon

Waterdrop Filter's K6 instant hot water RO System

Credit: Source: Waterdrop Filter

Multi-generational families have specific needs when it comes to their water accessibility. You want the highest quality of water for the most vulnerable people in your household: elderly family members who are prioritizing their health, and the littlest members of your family whose immune systems are still developing. The super versatile K6 RO system is hidden away under your sink and provides pure, five-stage RO filtered drinking water from its countertop-integrated faucet. Plus, the K6 can deliver instant filtered hot water, perfect for tea and preparing bottles of formula.

The K6’s smart touch display allows you to adjust your hot water temperature — all the way up to 203F — so you can get precisely what you need without the wait. Concerned parents will love the child lock, so you don’t need to worry about kids accessing hot water accidentally.

Like all of Waterdrop Filter’s products, the K6 RO system was designed to prioritize sustainability and performance, filling a cup in six seconds with 600GPD water flow while maintaining a 2:1 waste water ratio.

Waterdrop Filter K6
$499 $799 Save $300
Filtration stages
5
Flow speed
600GPD
Instant hot water
Yes

The Waterdrop K6 RO under-sink system combines five-stage pure filtration with the convenience of instant hot water, making it ideal for families with both young children and elderly members. With a smart touch display, child safety lock, 600GPD fast flow, and a sustainable 2:1 water ratio, it delivers safe, customizable, and eco-friendly hydration.

See at Amazon

Countertop convenience: Waterdrop Filter's A2 RO Hot-Cold Water Dispenser

Credit: Source: Waterdrop Filter

If you’re not ready to commit to an under-sink system, a portable countertop dispenser that still provides RO-filtered pure water is your best bet. Waterdrop Filter’s A2 countertop dispenser is a great option for renters, students who are living in dorm rooms, and anyone who needs the flexibility of a portable system. This installation-free, all-in-one dispenser uses Waterdrop Filter’s high-quality five-stage RO filtration system and can deliver instant hot water with six different temperature options at the touch of a button. In seconds, you can refill your iced lemon water before running out the door, make a hot cup of tea before your next meeting, or enjoy a glass of pure, room-temperature drinking water. Plus, the five pre-set water volume options make filling a breeze, so you get exactly what you need every time.

Waterdrop Filter A2
$379 $499 Save $120
Filtration stages
5
Instant hot water
Yes

The Waterdrop A2 countertop dispenser offers five-stage RO pure water in a portable, installation-free design perfect for renters, students, and small spaces. With instant hot water, six temperature settings, and five pre-set volume options, it delivers convenience, flexibility, and clean hydration at the touch of a button.

See at Amazon

Budget-friendly everyday solution: Waterdrop Filter's TSA 8-Layer Water Filter

Credit: Source: Waterdrop Filter

If you’re looking for a simple, cost-effective upgrade for your kitchen’s water source, the Waterdrop Filter's TSA 8-Layer Water Filter is a compact, convenient choice. This innovative under-sink filter system was specifically designed for municipal tap water, with a superior eight-stage filtration process that targets particles and sediment, rust, VOCs, chlorine, fluoride, and heavy metals. The slim, leak-proof TSA filter system is easy to install with your current plumbing and simple to remove — perfect for renters or anyone who’s not ready for a permanent installation. You get reliably pure drinking water straight from your existing tap without a major investment or complex installation process, making it the perfect first step for a budget-conscious family beginning its filtered-water journey.

Waterdrop Filter TSA 8 Layer
$94.99 $124.99 Save $30
Filtration stages
8
Instant hot water
No

The Waterdrop TSA 8-Layer Water Filter offers a simple, budget-friendly way to upgrade your tap water with advanced eight-stage filtration that reduces sediment, rust, VOCs, chlorine, fluoride, and heavy metals. Compact, leak-proof, and easy to install or remove, it’s ideal for renters and families seeking pure drinking water without a long-term commitment.

See at Amazon

Why choose Waterdrop Filter this Prime Day?

Waterdrop Filter has built its reputation and earned its customer allegiance through a dedication to water safety, a continuous pursuit of innovation, and by providing products that fit seamlessly into myriad everyday lives, no matter your living situation or budget. This Fall Prime Day, Waterdrop Filter is offering unbeatable deals on its best-selling products. Imagine a cozier, healthier fall with your favorite hot drinks and guaranteed pure water. Whether it’s just for you or for an entire, multi-generational family, the peace of mind that comes with consuming purified drinking water will always be worth it.

For more Prime Day deal products, please check the Waterdrop Filter Prime Day Page on Amazon and on their official website. Don’t miss these limited-time opportunities — from October 7-8 — to invest in your long-term health and well-being by consuming pure drinking water every day. This season, make every sip you take pure, safe, and satisfying with Waterdrop Filter.

This article is sponsored by Waterdrop Filter.

Yes

The Waterdrop A2 countertop dispenser offers five-stage RO pure water in a portable, installation-free design perfect for renters, students, and small spaces. With instant hot water, six temperature settings, and five pre-set volume options, it delivers convenience, flexibility, and clean hydration at the touch of a button.

See at Amazon

Budget-friendly everyday solution: Waterdrop Filter's TSA 8-Layer Water Filter

Credit: Source: Waterdrop Filter

If you’re looking for a simple, cost-effective upgrade for your kitchen’s water source, the Waterdrop Filter's TSA 8-Layer Water Filter is a compact, convenient choice. This innovative under-sink filter system was specifically designed for municipal tap water, with a superior eight-stage filtration process that targets particles and sediment, rust, VOCs, chlorine, fluoride, and heavy metals. The slim, leak-proof TSA filter system is easy to install with your current plumbing and simple to remove — perfect for renters or anyone who’s not ready for a permanent installation. You get reliably pure drinking water straight from your existing tap without a major investment or complex installation process, making it the perfect first step for a budget-conscious family beginning its filtered-water journey.

8
Instant hot water
No

The Waterdrop TSA 8-Layer Water Filter offers a simple, budget-friendly way to upgrade your tap water with advanced eight-stage filtration that reduces sediment, rust, VOCs, chlorine, fluoride, and heavy metals. Compact, leak-proof, and easy to install or remove, it’s ideal for renters and families seeking pure drinking water without a long-term commitment.

See at Amazon

Waterdrop Filter has built its reputation and earned its customer allegiance through a dedication to water safety, a continuous pursuit of innovation, and by providing products that fit seamlessly into myriad everyday lives, no matter your living situation or budget. Whether it’s just for you or for an entire, multi-generational family, the peace of mind that comes with consuming purified drinking water will always be worth it.

For more Prime Day deal products, please check the Waterdrop Filter Prime Day Page on Amazon and on their official website. Don’t miss these limited-time opportunities — from October 7-8 — to invest in your long-term health and well-being by consuming pure drinking water every day. This season, make every sip you take pure, safe, and satisfying with Waterdrop Filter.

item.content:encoded:
Fall 2025’s Prime Big Deal Days is the perfect time to jump on some amazing sales and invest in home essentials that will support your long-term health. What better way to commit to your well-being than with everyday, healthy hydration? From October 7-8 Waterdrop Filter is offering up to $700 off its premium water purification systems, ranging from its high-end, flagship under-sink systems that can support large families to its ultra-convenient countertop models designed for small spaces.

With cozy season just around the corner, Waterdrop Filter can help you elevate your seasonal beverages: instant, steaming-hot tea; hot cocoa for the kids made with guaranteed pure water; fresh, immunity-boosting citrus water to help ward off circulating flu bugs; ice-cold filtered water to keep you going through your fall workout challenge.

Waterdrop Filter is a long-trusted brand that has dedicated itself to water safety and developing innovative water purification systems to enhance your everyday life. Pure, great-tasting water shouldn’t be a luxury — it’s a necessity that everyone should have access to. Not only is Waterdrop Filter focused on providing healthier water, but it’s also on a mission to decrease plastic waste, and to date has replaced a staggering 1 billion+ single-use plastic water bottles.

Why pure drinking water matters this season

We all know what’s lurking around the corner in the fall and winter: the dreaded cold and flu bugs that circulate when we all move inside for the season. If you want to support a healthy immune system, start with the basics: pure filtered water.

Waterdrop Filter products use an advanced, multi-step Reverse Osmosis (RO) filtration system to reduce large particles, reduce dissolved solids and contaminants, and improve your water’s taste with a post-carbon filter. You end up with safe, better-tasting water with fewer contaminants. Whether you’re preparing your baby’s bottle with formula, cooking a seasonal soup, or indulging in a rainy afternoon Americano, Waterdrop Filter provides premium filtration for the highest-quality, guaranteed pure water every time.

Waterdrop Filter’s premium under-sink RO systems

Waterdrop Filter's X12 Pro RO System

Credit: Waterdrop Filter

If your family refuses to compromise on convenience and the quality of its drinking water, Waterdrop Filter’s flagship X12 Pro RO under-sink system is the perfect fit. The tankless RO system easily slides under your sink, with dual flow that connects to your main faucet as well as the countertop-integrated RO faucet. From your existing faucet, you can now enjoy five-stage filtered water for everyday activities such as washing dishes and rinsing fruits and vegetables. From your new X12 Pro faucet you have the purest, mineral-fortified drinking water that has gone through Waterdrop Filter’s superior 11-stage under-sink RO system.

Busy families will appreciate the speedy, 1200GPD rapid flow that can fill a cup in a mere three seconds. Whether you’re filling a large pot of water for pasta or prepping the kids’ water bottles before school, using the RO faucet won’t slow you down. Unlike traditional RO systems, the X12 Pro was built with both performance and sustainability in mind, providing a 3:1 water ratio that reduces waste water while maintaining a quick flow.

Waterdrop Filter X12 Pro
$999 $1399 Save $400
Filtration stages
11
Flow speed
1200GPD
Instant hot water
No

The Waterdrop X12 Pro RO under-sink system delivers convenience and quality with dual-flow filtration, providing five-stage filtered water from your main faucet and 11-stage mineral-fortified drinking water from its dedicated RO faucet. With rapid 1200GPD flow and a sustainable 3:1 water ratio, it’s designed to keep up with busy families while minimizing waste.

See at Amazon

Waterdrop Filter's K6 instant hot water RO System

Credit: Source: Waterdrop Filter

Multi-generational families have specific needs when it comes to their water accessibility. You want the highest quality of water for the most vulnerable people in your household: elderly family members who are prioritizing their health, and the littlest members of your family whose immune systems are still developing. The super versatile K6 RO system is hidden away under your sink and provides pure, five-stage RO filtered drinking water from its countertop-integrated faucet. Plus, the K6 can deliver instant filtered hot water, perfect for tea and preparing bottles of formula.

The K6’s smart touch display allows you to adjust your hot water temperature — all the way up to 203F — so you can get precisely what you need without the wait. Concerned parents will love the child lock, so you don’t need to worry about kids accessing hot water accidentally.

Like all of Waterdrop Filter’s products, the K6 RO system was designed to prioritize sustainability and performance, filling a cup in six seconds with 600GPD water flow while maintaining a 2:1 waste water ratio.

The Waterdrop K6 RO under-sink system combines five-stage pure filtration with the convenience of instant hot water, making it ideal for families with both young children and elderly members. With a smart touch display, child safety lock, 600GPD fast flow, and a sustainable 2:1 water ratio, it delivers safe, customizable, and eco-friendly hydration.

See at Amazon

Countertop convenience: Waterdrop Filter's A2 RO Hot-Cold Water Dispenser

Credit: Source: Waterdrop Filter

If you’re not ready to commit to an under-sink system, a portable countertop dispenser that still provides RO-filtered pure water is your best bet. Waterdrop Filter’s A2 countertop dispenser is a great option for renters, students who are living in dorm rooms, and anyone who needs the flexibility of a portable system. This installation-free, all-in-one dispenser uses Waterdrop Filter’s high-quality five-stage RO filtration system and can deliver instant hot water with six different temperature options at the touch of a button. In seconds, you can refill your iced lemon water before running out the door, make a hot cup of tea before your next meeting, or enjoy a glass of pure, room-temperature drinking water. item.title: This $749 laptop is an all-in-one beast for play and productivity
item.itunes:title: This $749 laptop is an all-in-one beast for play and productivity
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Acer Nitro V 16
$749 $1049 Save $300
CPU
AMD Ryzen 7
GPU
Nvidia GeForce RTX 5050
Display (Size, Resolution)
16-inch, 1920 x 1200
RAM
16GB
Storage
512GB

This laptop checks all the right boxes if you're trying to get one that can handle creative work, gaming and everything in between for a fair price. While it's normally over $1,000, it can now be had for just $749, which is well below its normal retail.

Operating System
Windows
$749 at Walmart

It's not easy to find a powerful laptop with dedicated graphics if you're on a small budget. Which is why we think it's worth taking a look at this Acer laptop that's now just $749. You get a large 16-inch screen with a refresh rate of 180Hz, a powerful AMD Ryzen 7 chip, and an Nvidia GeForce RTX 5050 graphics card.

It's perfect for doing everyday work, playing games, or even throwing, pushing it a little harder with creative tasks. We're confident that this laptop will be able to handle everything you throw at it. And even if it's a little more than what you think you'll need, we still think it's going to be a good option.

Lots of power, small price

For the price, this is an impressive laptop that delivers on many levels. We like that it has a powerful processor, which is also backed up by a good amount of RAM and storage. As far as the details go, you're looking at the AMD Ryzen 7 260 chip, 16GB of DDR5 RAM, and a 512GB SSD.

In addition to the raw power above, this laptop is equipped to handle AI workflows as well. In addition, the Nvidia GeForce RTX 5050 does most of the heavy lifting, whenever you need extra muscle for any graphical work. This can be for work projects, creative apps, and also games.

Acer's MUX Switch gives you the ability to control when you're relying on the integrated graphics, and when you're utilizing the dedicated graphics card. It's a nice feature to have, especially if you're looking to conserve battery life while on the go. While the RTX 5050 isn't the beefiest Nvidia graphics card on the market right now, it'll be enough for most people.

Credit: Acer

If you want to get something for gaming, we suggest looking for a laptop dedicated to that. You'll get better hardware, but it will also come at a higher price. The Acer Nitro V 16 provides a good balance between performance and price. We don't think you'll be disappointed with what this laptop can achieve. And as you might expect from a gaming laptop, there's also a backlit keyboard, along with a webcam, and a decent set of built-in speakers.

And if you're thinking about building this laptop out, that's also a possibility. The laptop features a variety of ports, with three USB-A, USB-C, HDMI, Ethernet, a microSD card slot, and a 3.5mm audio jack. There's also Wi-Fi 6E and Bluetooth. It's really surprising what you're getting for this price. But you'll want to decide quickly because a promotion like this won't be around for long.

You can grab this discounted laptop from Walmart. The only catch is that it's exclusive to Walmart+ members for now. However, the sale will open to the public as well, so if you're a little patient, you'll still be able to claim the discount. The brand does offer a 30-day trial of Walmart+, which means you can take advantage of all the special savings that the retailer has to offer, too.

item.title: Forget buying a larger CPU cooler, a contact frame may be all you need
item.itunes:title: Forget buying a larger CPU cooler, a contact frame may be all you need
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When building or upgrading your PC, you likely connected your cooler directly to your CPU using a factory mounting bracket, with a generous layer of thermal paste between the CPU and cooler. In most cases, that's all you need to keep your PC running cool, and that's probably the setup you're used to building. However, if your CPU is having issues staying cool, despite purchasing the appropriate cooler for your build, you may want to consider adding a cheap accessory to your motherboard.

Rather than spending a significant amount of money on a larger, more robust cooler, you may be able to resolve your issue with a relatively inexpensive addition to your CPU. That's right; you might just need a contact frame.

Contact frames

What is a CPU contact frame?

Contact frames are a CPU mounting bracket that replaces the motherboard's stock integrated loading mechanism. A contact frame can prevent the CPU from bending under pressure and offers better contact between the CPU's integrated heat spreader and the CPU cooler, which can offer a significant reduction in CPU temperatures and improve cooling performance.

A contact frame optimizes your cooling by providing a flatter IHS surface for your CPU cooler, allowing the CPU to make better and larger contact with it. This offers a more efficient path for heat dissipation and can improve your cooling performance by up to 12%.

It's not just about cooling the CPU

Better cooling comes with fringe benefits

Contact frames aren't just about cooling your CPU better, though. Contact frames started becoming more popular on PC builds with Intel's LGA-1700 socket CPUs, spanning the 12th through 14th generations. Due to the size of these CPUs, they may struggle with bending over time, particularly when the heat sink is located in the socket. Due to inconsistent pressure points and cooling management, the CPUs could bend over time, which would cause the CPU to bend away from the contact points on the motherboard.

While not every CPU in Intel's 12th-13th generation suffered from warping, a contact frame can give you some additional peace of mind. Especially if you're overclocking the CPU.

Due to the same rectangular shape of Intel's Arrow Lake and (presumably) Arrow Lake Refresh chipsets, contact frames can also be helpful for LGA-1851 socket CPUs.

While most Intel CPU designs don't suffer from the same heat-sink location flaw as Intel's 12th-14th generation, a contact frame will help even out the pressure of your CPU socket mount, regardless of which generation you decide to use in your PC build.

AMD CPUs are still square and don't suffer the same warping as newer Intel CPUs, though a contact frame may just help with slightly better cooling management on the AM5 socket. A contact frame can also make it easier to clean and reapply thermal paste the next time you decide to upgrade your rig, which is handy for Intel and AMD enthusiasts.

How to know you need a contact frame

Look out for some key warning signs

Technically, no CPUs need a contact frame. However, they are pretty highly recommended for those running newer Intel CPUs from the 12th generation onward, due to the elongated design of the LGA-1700 and LGA-1851 sockets, and due to the potential for CPU warping on those chips.

However, if you're looking into a contact frame for better heat management, you'll be able to tell you need one if your CPU runs hot while stress testing. While CPUs can operate at high temperatures, exposure to prolonged high temperatures can accelerate the end of life for your processor. If you're experiencing thermal throttling, random crashes and shutdowns, or high-temperature warnings from your CPU temperature monitoring software, it's essential to review your cooling options, including a contact frame.

If your CPU cooler is unable to withstand the load of your CPU at high capacity, you may be dealing with poor contact between your CPU and cooler, which is exactly the kind of issue a contact frame can resolve. While obtaining an overpowered cooler can help alleviate this issue, it doesn't address the underlying problem, which is a poor fit between your CPU and cooling mount. As a result, it's a waste of money and cooling power compared to a $10-$20 contact frame.

Contact frames are not a cure-all

Don't rule out a cooler upgrade if you're experiencing serious thermal issues

The benefits of a contact frame can help you with thermal issues, but you're mostly going to get 10% better cooling or less with the contact frame, though the degree to which a contact frame can help your cooler is dependent on the CPU and cooler in question, as well as your overclock profile and workloads. Some will only see a 2–3% increase in thermal management, while others will get higher benefits.

You can combine a contact frame with additional thermal management options, such as setting custom fan curves or using a more advanced fan controller, for a multi-pronged approach to enhanced cooling.

However, if you've got serious thermal issues with your rig, a contact frame isn't the way to go. You may still want to invest in one when you switch to a new cooler, as it's a cost-effective addition, and the worst it can do is not impact your performance at all. However, if your PC is thermal throttling at low load, you have bigger issues than a contact frame can fix.

Better cooling comes with fringe benefits

Contact frames aren't just about cooling your CPU better, though. Contact frames started becoming more popular on PC builds with Intel's LGA-1700 socket CPUs, spanning the 12th through 14th generations. Due to the size of these CPUs, they may struggle with bending over time, particularly when the heat sink is located in the socket. Due to inconsistent pressure points and cooling management, the CPUs could bend over time, which would cause the CPU to bend away from the contact points on the motherboard.

While not every CPU in Intel's 12th-13th generation suffered from warping, a contact frame can give you some additional peace of mind. Especially if you're overclocking the CPU.

Due to the same rectangular shape of Intel's Arrow Lake and (presumably) Arrow Lake Refresh chipsets, contact frames can also be helpful for LGA-1851 socket CPUs.

While most Intel CPU designs don't suffer from the same heat-sink location flaw as Intel's 12th-14th generation, a contact frame will help even out the pressure of your CPU socket mount, regardless of which generation you decide to use in your PC build.

AMD CPUs are still square and don't suffer the same warping as newer Intel CPUs, though a contact frame may just help with slightly better cooling management on the AM5 socket. A contact frame can also make it easier to clean and reapply thermal paste the next time you decide to upgrade your rig, which is handy for Intel and AMD enthusiasts.

How to know you need a contact frame

Look out for some key warning signs

Technically, no CPUs need a contact frame. However, they are pretty highly recommended for those running newer Intel CPUs from the 12th generation onward, due to the elongated design of the LGA-1700 and LGA-1851 sockets, and due to the potential for CPU warping on those chips.

However, if you're looking into a contact frame for better heat management, you'll be able to tell you need one if your CPU runs hot while stress testing. While CPUs can operate at high temperatures, exposure to prolonged high temperatures can accelerate the end of life for your processor. If you're experiencing thermal throttling, random crashes and shutdowns, or high-temperature warnings from your CPU temperature monitoring software, it's essential to review your cooling options, including a contact frame.

If your CPU cooler is unable to withstand the load of your CPU at high capacity, you may be dealing with poor contact between your CPU and cooler, which is exactly the kind of issue a contact frame can resolve. While obtaining an overpowered cooler can help alleviate this issue, it doesn't address the underlying problem, which is a poor fit between your CPU and cooling mount. As a result, it's a waste of money and cooling power compared to a $10-$20 contact frame.

Contact frames are not a cure-all

Don't rule out a cooler upgrade if you're experiencing serious thermal issues

The benefits of a contact frame can help you with thermal issues, but you're mostly going to get 10% better cooling or less with the contact frame, though the degree to which a contact frame can help your cooler is dependent on the CPU and cooler in question, as well as your overclock profile and workloads. Some will only see a 2–3% increase in thermal management, while others will get higher benefits.

You can combine a contact frame with additional thermal management options, such as setting custom fan curves or using a more advanced fan controller, for a multi-pronged approach to enhanced cooling.

However, if you've got serious thermal issues with your rig, a contact frame isn't the way to go. You may still want to invest in one when you switch to a new cooler, as it's a cost-effective addition, and the worst it can do is not impact your performance at all. However, if your PC is thermal throttling at low load, you have bigger issues than a contact frame can fix.
